rescue_reasoning: Keep because the policy is explicitly circular/resource-efficiency oriented. Verbatim states: 'reduce, reuse, and repair, by requiring take-back and resale of used clothing, and promoting donation and longevity of used apparel and textiles.' These measures extend product lifetime and reduce waste/material demand (matches canonical mechanism 'textile_reuse_and_takeback → waste_generation_reduction'). Gate failure ('no Resource Efficiency outcome token found') appears to be a tokenization issue rather than lack of climate/resource-efficiency substance. climate_screen='explicit_self' supports inclusion.
policy_statement: By 2023, require take-back and resale of used clothing and promote donation and longevity of apparel/textiles (RPC.3-2).
mechanism_description: It requires clothing take-back and resale and promotes donation and longer-lasting apparel so fewer textiles are discarded, reducing overall waste generation and associated emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Same clothing service (wear/use) → ↓ demand for virgin textile production + ↓ discarded textiles → ↓ energy/material throughput → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes the textiles system by requiring/encouraging take-back, resale, donation, and longer product lifetimes, shifting consumption from “buy new, discard” to reuse/extend-life. (2) This reduces material extraction and energy-intensive manufacturing of new fibers/apparel and lowers waste flows to landfills/incinerators. (3) The climate outcome is lower lifecycle GHG emissions (primarily from avoided production; secondarily from reduced disposal-related emissions and waste logistics).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoided GHG emissions from reduced production of new textiles due to increased reuse, repair, and resale.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: providing the same clothing function with fewer new goods through reuse/take-back, thereby reducing material and energy throughput and associated lifecycle emissions.  
2) Why Resource Efficiency over Mitigation: while the endpoint is lower GHGs, the defining mechanism is demand reduction/efficiency (using existing textiles longer) rather than direct fuel-switching or end-of-pipe abatement.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a policy focuses mainly on landfill methane avoidance via diversion, it could look like Mitigation; however, for textile take-back/reuse the upstream avoided production effect generally dominates and is inherent to the mechanism.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: any policy that increases textile reuse/take-back to reduce textile discards necessarily reduces material throughput and typically reduces lifecycle emissions via avoided new production, fitting Resource Efficiency.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., whether emissions reductions come more from avoided manufacturing vs avoided disposal) is deferred to policy-level evidence and local waste management context.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If implementation is primarily “take-back for recycling” without meaningful displacement of new textile production (low reuse, high downcycling) or if disposal emissions dominate locally (e.g., high-incineration systems), the dominant effect could shift toward direct waste-sector mitigation; nevertheless, the stated mechanism centers on reuse/longevity, supporting Resource Efficiency.
key_indicators: "By 2023"; "requiring take-back and resale of used clothing"; "reduce, reuse, and repair"; "promoting donation and longevity of used apparel and textiles"
co_benefits: Reduced landfill/incineration and local pollution; waste management cost savings; job creation in repair/resale; consumer savings; reduced resource extraction and water use impacts from textile production.
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes a promotional/encouragement element (“promoting donation and longevity”) that could be enabling/informational on its own, but the presence of “requiring take-back and resale” makes the overall instrument a direct regulatory mandate.
